data.title: CALM Chain International Seeks IPO At Excessive Valuation And High Risks
data.description:
CALM Chain International is Growing, But Risks Are High

CALM Chain International Limited (CCIL) has filed to raise $18 million in an IPO of its Class A ordinary shares, per an F-1 registration statement.

The company provides a range of human resource outsourcing services in Hong Kong.

CCIL is growing but has an uneven operating history, high risks, thin capitalization, and excessive valuation assumptions at IPO.

My outlook is to sell (avoid) the CCIL IPO.

What Does CALM Chain Do?

CCIL operates as Talent Quest in Hong Kong and was founded in 2012 to provide various human resource outsourcing services primarily focused on finding and retaining qualified employees for small and medium-sized businesses in the greater Hong Kong region.

The company is led by founder, Chairman, and CEO Mr. Andrew Teh Yao, who is also a venture partner and mentor at Inspect Ventures Fund, a seed capital fund.

CALM Chain offers three primary services:

  • Employer of Record outsourcing services

  • Executive search services

  • Human resources research services.

A breakdown of the revenue contribution from each of these service offering segments is shown in the pie chart below:

The company's client base includes

multinational companies and governmental organizations, regional branch offices in Hong Kong, financial institutions, construction companies, fintech company and other SMEs.

Talent Quest also maintains a website for job postings and a skills database to help match job openings with prospective personnel.

Employee Compensation as a percentage of revenue has fallen as revenue has increased, per the table below:

(Source - SEC.)

What is CALM Chain’s Market?

The company operates in the HR professional services market.

According to a 2026 market research report by Mordor Intelligence, the Asia Pacific region HR professional services was an estimated $134 billion in 2025 and was expected to exceed $203 billion by the end of 2030.

If it achieved this growth, would represent an 8.63% CAGR from 2026 to 2030, a moderate rate of growth for an already large industry.

CALM Chain operates in a subset of the industry and in one smaller region, Hong Kong.

The industry is adopting digital-oriented HR solutions, such as cloud-based HR management software, amid a growing demand for outsourced HR services, such as payroll processing, recruiting, and compliance.

The company faces intense competition from competitors of all sizes, including global full-service firms, boutique specialists, local Hong Kong agencies, and in-house corporate recruiting teams.

Also, sectors such as government and banking clients often have long tendering processes, which makes for higher barriers to entry in some cases.

The company's strengths include long-term relationships with certain clients, a proprietary candidate database, and a comprehensive suite of services.

Weaknesses include high client concentration and regional location concentration as well as reliance on a small number of key personnel in the company.

Its business opportunities include the potential to expand into Southeast Asian countries such as Vietnam, Malaysia, and Indonesia and integrating AI technologies to automate candidate matching to streamline administrative tasks.

Threats to CALM’s prospects include intense and varied market competition with generally low barriers to entry and an uncertain regulatory environment.

CALM Chain’s Recent Financial Results

CCIL’s revenue has grown materially from a small base, while it has begun to produce a profit on higher gross margin.

The firm’s gross margin has increased while its Employee & Compensation costs as a percentage of revenue have fallen, as shown in the chart here:

CALM Chain’s IPO Plan

CCIL intends to sell three million shares of Class A stock at a proposed midpoint price of $6.00 per share.

Immediately after the IPO, the firm’s enterprise value will approximate $111 million.

Dilution to new shareholders will be $5.46, and the percentage share float at IPO will be 15%.

Class A shareholders (the public) will have one vote per share.

Class B shareholder and company founder/Chairman Teh will have twenty (20) votes per share and will have voting control of the company immediately after the IPO, so the company will be a ‘controlled company’ according to Nasdaq’s rules.

Net proceeds from the IPO will be used by the company for:

approximately 30% for business development and expansion into the Asia Pacific market;

approximately 30% for applying artificial intelligence technology into the business for resources optimization; and

approximately 40% for general administration and working capital purposes.

(Source - SEC.)

The table below shows the company's primary metrics pre- and post-IPO:

CALM Chain is Growing and Profitable, but Risks Are High

The company is seeking to raise $18 million, as the minimum hurdle for foreign IPOs has moved to $15 million.

CCIL’s revenue has grown from a small base, and it has most recently generated a profit on higher gross margin.

Cash flow from operations has also turned positive in the most recent fiscal year on no capital expenditures, which may not be sustainable for long.

However, the company faces numerous risks, including its small size and thin capitalization in an intensely competitive market.

The firm is also a "foreign private issuer" and an "emerging growth company," which allows management to disclose significantly less information to shareholders.

Many of these types of company stocks have performed poorly after their IPO, with Asian companies producing particularly poor results in many cases.

While the market growth dynamics for HR outsourcing services is positive, the fact that the company is already discussing expanding away from Hong Kong despite generating very little revenue indicates its lack of confidence in its home market as well as high risks in operating in different cultural contexts.

Its U.S. listing also contains risks, as Chinese and U.S. regulators have yet to settle on a regulatory agreement for cross-border listings, and the firm may be subject to unpredictable regulatory changes in either country, which could hurt investors in the process.

Also, Hong Kong’s status as a special autonomous region within the larger Mainland Chinese legal and financial system is uncertain as to its trajectory, with the ongoing possibility of political changes and social unrest there increasing the risks for companies operating in that region.

While CCIL is growing and now profitable, its previous results were loss-generating, so the company shows no consistent history of profits or growth.

Management is also asking IPO investors to pay an EV/EBITDA multiple of over 85x, an extremely high figure for a company with such an uneven operating history.

Given the firm’s many risks, thin capitalization, and excessive valuation assumption, my outlook on the CCIL IPO is to sell (avoid it).

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced.

data.title: Taiwan Semiconductor: Transition From An iPhone To An AI Era
data.description:
TSM stock: Q4 2025 marks a pivot point

I last analyzed Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) on Dec 16, 2025. That article, titled " Taiwan Semiconductor: This Is The GARP Moment You've Waited For,” argued for a Buy rating on TSM. More details of that article were shown in the screenshot below.

Since then, the company has released its earnings report (ER) for FQ4 2025 on January 15, 2026. As a battlefront chip stock, the financials have been quickly dissected by many analysts. As such, in this article, I will skip most of the background and directly get to my point. In a nutshell, I believe that TSM has reached a historic pivot point in Q4, which marks transition of its business model from mobile handset to AI chips as its primary growth engine.

As most TSM investor must know, Apple (AAPL) used to be the largest customer for TSM. Even as recent as mid-2024 (see the next chart below), Apple’s contribution to TSM’s total revenues exceeded the second largest contributor, Nvidia (NVDA), by more than twofold.

However, based on the financials released for Q4 and also the NVDA CEO’s comments shortly afterward (see the news release below), NVDA has now overtaken Apple as TSM’s largest customer.

Yahoo! News: Nvidia Corp's (NASDAQ:NVDA) surge in artificial intelligence demand is reshaping the global semiconductor supply chain. Nvidia has overtaken Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL) as Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. Ltd's (NYSE:TSM) largest customer. Nvidia chief Jensen Huang confirmed the change publicly for the first time during a recent podcast.

I expect this shift to have profound implications for TSM growth and also valuation, for reasons detailed next.

TSM stock: growth outlook in the AI era

To start, I expect the transition to help TSM to further decouple from the seasonal volatility of the iPhone cycle. Instead, its future growth will be re-anchored in the infrastructure of artificial intelligence and related applications. These changes should quicken the growth of its leading-edge node technologies (such as the demand for 3nm and 5nm nodes) as the deployment of high-performance computing (HPC) becomes more pervasive. These developments should not only fuel more consistent growth but also widen TSM’s profit margins in the years ahead in my model.

As a reflection of my above assessment, consensus also sees a strong growth curve in the years ahead. To wit, the chart below shows the current consensus EPS estimates for TSM. As seen, the estimates point to robust growth rates of 34% and 26% YOY for the next two years. Overall, the EPS is expected to grow at a rate of 11.1% CAGR in the next 3 years.

The above estimate is in reasonable agreement with my own projections based on the company’s long-term ROCE (return on capital employed) and growth reinvestment rates (GRR). Details of these concepts are described in my earlier articles and only the finals results are quoted here:

The ROCE for TSM has been improving rapidly in recent years as seen in the chart below. I expect its 2025 ROCE to reach about 55.4%. As for its GRR, I expect it to be around 20% on average in the next few years given its growth CAPEX plans as updated in the FQ4 earning report. With these inputs, TSM’s organic growth rate would be 11% (54.4% ROCE x 20% RR = 11%) by my model.

TSM stock: valuation is still attractive

Two caveats about my above growth projections are worth note. On the positive side, I consider my above estimates to be on the conservative side as it does not factor in the margin expansion from AI chips as just mentioned. My estimates above are largely based on an extrapolation of its historical ROCE, which is heavily influenced by the contribution of smartphone components. On the negative side, I don't expect the company's GRR to be sustainable at 20% permanently. For mature companies, my experience is that a sustainable GRR would be around 10%.

To incorporate the above caveats, here I will use a 2-stage free cash flow (FCF) model to estimate the company’s fair price. The model was detailed in another article (which applied the model to Microsoft (MSFT)), and the key parameters in this model are:

There are a total of 3 key parameters in the 2-stage discounted FCF: the discount rate, the growth rate in stage 1, and the terminal growth rate in stage 2. For the discount rate, I relied on the so-called WACC, the weighted average cost of the capital model. The discount rate for TSM is about 9.43% on average in recent years following this model.

For TSM’s growth rate in stage 1, I plugged in the 11% rate based on both consensus estimates and my own projection detailed in the forgoing section. For the growth rate in stage 2, I assumed a 55.4% ROCE with a GRR of 10%, resulting in a terminal growth rate of around 5.54%.

Finally, my estimate for its FWD FCF is around $10.55 per share, With all these parameters, the table below summarizes my analysis of its fair valuation. two-stage discounted FCF. As seen, discounted FCF model shows a fair price of around $430, leaving a mild margin of safety (compared to its current share price of around $360) despite the large price appreciation since my last writing.

TSM stock: other risks and final thoughts

The stock certainly entails some downside risks. To start, the valuation metrics appear to be elevated either by horizontal or vertical comparison. For example, the chart below compares a verity of its P/E ratios against the sector median and its own historical averages. As seen, TSM's current TTM P/E is about 34x. This is above the sector median of 23.6 by a large premium of more than 43% and its own five-year average of 24.7 by more than 37%. However, if you subscribe to my thesis about its business transitioning into a new era and could enjoy structurally better profitable metrics, then you probably can agree that its own past P/E ratios offer limited guidance for its future valuation multiples.

As a second negative associated with the transition to AI chips, it may require a learning curve, which could be extended, for TSM to perfect its manufacturing yields and materialize on the margin potential. AI chips, as impressive as the growth in recent years, still lack the sheer unit volume that is needed to overcome the learning curve. Over the iPhone era, smartphone chips are produced by the hundreds of millions, providing a massive amount experience that allows TSMC to tweak its manufacturing yield, minimize costs, and optimize profits.

But in the end, my overall assessment is that these negatives should be ultimately temporary. The secular trend I am seeing is a net positive. I expect the AI era to offer TSM a structurally superior financial profile compared to the smartphone era, characterized by higher demand, higher pricing power, more resilient margins, and a more consistent growth trajectory that is no longer beholden to consumer replacement cycles. These considerations have led me to maintain my bullish rating despite the valuation expansion since my last writing(s).

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: S&P 500: Picking Winners And Losers In The 20 Biggest Names
data.description:
This might be both my favorite stock screen and my favorite way to use Seeking Alpha's equity data and quant factor grades. I am a self-proclaimed Dow Industrials fan. But even I will admit that while that diverse set of 30 stocks is a great diversified shopping list for large-cap equity names, the Dow is not "the market" like it used to be.

And frankly, neither is the S&P 500. Not when most of the stocks in it don't move the needle on index performance. Here are SPY's top 10 holdings. A third of the weighting is technology, no surprise. But the 10 biggest stocks make up 36% of the index.

How skewed is it at the top? The next 10 (the 11th to 20th largest S&P 500 components) only add another 10% to the total, bringing the top 20 right around half the total index. The other 50% of the S&P 500? 450 stocks!

So this is not a market of stocks; it's a stock market that is driven by perhaps 2 dozen names. And while it's great to see some recent broadening beyond those behemoths, it is similar to the evolution of US government debt: it took so long to get here, it won't just go back in the bottle like the proverbial genie.

For those who say, "But it's always been this way," check this out. As it turns out, a more "normal" level is around 30-35% historically.

Either way, my point is that if you are trying to figure out what the market is doing, you had better know what's happening with the top 20. So let's do that.

What the 20 Biggest S&P 500 Stocks Look Like Right Now

This is a screen I maintain for quick retrieval. The iShares Top 20 US Stocks ETF (TOPT) tracks them. However, that's an ETF that has periodic rebalance dates, so it won't always be current with the top 20. For instance, since the last time I pulled this up myself, Netflix (NFLX) and AbbVie (ABBV) are out of the top 20, and Micron (MU) and Bank of America (BAC) are in. That's not some committee decision, just a run of most recent market caps (2/9/26 market close).

I'm a technician, and after decades of that as my focus, the way I use this information is as follows, as indicated with timely examples/highlights from this data:

  1. That's a quality group right there. Profitability grades are in the A category, except for JP Morgan (JPM). But that, I assume, is more about the way it is calculated. Unless they are secretly a modern Bear Stearns in disguise (yes, they did absorb that broken firm during the GFC), I'm not losing sleep over JP Morgan's statistical deficiency in that area.
  2. Valuation is what I'd expect, given my sanguine market view. F and D grades dominate. Micron scores the lone B grade, and BAC and Berkshire (BRK.B) at least land in the C range. So this is an overvalued market from a bottom-up standpoint. And remember, that translates to the full S&P 500, unless the other 480 stocks are averaging a solid B or higher. That is not the case. P/E Ratios are the opposite of slim on a trailing basis. Forward estimates are more tame, but they are estimates, not in the books yet.
  3. Growth is mixed, and that seems to be a story in this year's stock market. AI is spending, and big stocks in tech are growing. And as you can see, momentum in the stock prices follows the growth, as there's a decent correlation between the Growth and Momentum grades in the table.
  4. Note that there's an inherent bias when it comes to price momentum. Some stocks have been so big for so long, they could drop by a lot and remain in the S&P 500's top 20. Others reach the top 20 because they recently surged in price. Again, MU is a prime example
  5. The YTD performance has been spotty, as we know from watching the headlines. But what might not be as clear to many investors is how many stocks in this top 20 have really not done well the past 12 months. 8 of the 20 names are down or are up no more than 5% during that period.

BOTTOM LINE: This market is not so uniformly great or awful that we can't try to identify some potential winners and losers. So that's what I'll turn to the charts to do, projecting over a 4-6 month time frame or so.

That is, I'll use weekly charts. Daily charts are more popular, but they also tend to have too much "noise" in them, especially around earnings season, as is the case now. The weeklies hopefully smooth out the bumps and provide a clearer picture for us.

As my regular readers know, my charting style is a bit different than most. I am primarily interested in avoiding big losses along the way to lower-volatility profits. I do plenty of hedging to accomplish that. But for this article, let's pretend I don't. So I can simply take more of the type of view that I used to hear my Wall Street friends talk about a few decades ago: their job as portfolio managers was not to avoid big loss.

It was to beat the S&P 500 index using a fully invested stock portfolio. So back then, if they used sectors like Utilities (not represented among the current top 20 stocks), it was not because they loved those stocks. It was because they were not permitted by mandate to own a lot of cash or bonds or use hedging techniques.

With that specified, it's down to my favorite part, the charts. There's not enough space allotted to chart all 20. So instead I've chosen to highlight the 3 best and 3 worst I see at this moment. Earnings can swing that around a bit, but that's out of our control.

I did set out, as I normally do, to identify those in the top 20 that I estimate to have at least a 60% chance of moving up by 10% before they move down by 10%. That's my go-to return/risk tradeoff. I'd prefer 70%, but this market doesn't afford that.

Looking for Strong Stocks...looking...looking

Now, the sad part. I only found 3 that I analyze to have more than a 50% chance, based on my chart analysis. So it's a good thing I didn't promise more than that!

And as you'd expect, when looking for the "losers," those I judge to have no more than a 30% chance of gaining 10% before declining 10% from their current prices, there's a lot to choose from. In fact, 8 of the 20 stocks are what I'd call "elevated risk," whereas only 3 are "below average risk." The other 9 are less clear, which is par for the course in any analysis, technical or otherwise.

As a reminder to those less familiar with my investment process, honed over 30+ years in my former career managing private client wealth and a few mutual funds, I am very much against the traditional "buy-sell-hold" thing. Why? Because those terms are meaningless, since they only cover the WHAT portion of the portfolio decision, not the HOW MUCH aspect.

Put more simply, I can own any of these 20 stocks with 1% of my portfolio. If it fell 30% on earnings tomorrow, I would not be happy. But I would be, sort of. Because it only cost me 0.30% for every $100,000 in my portfolio, that $1,000 position would have lost $300 on that catastrophic day.

I think in "basis points of impact," not "buy or sell." Because on the flip side of that, if I own something with, say, 7% of my portfolio, and it falls by just 5% from cost, guess what? My basis point impact is 0.35%. That's $350 on every $100,000 in the portfolio.

So ratings mean very little to me without the portfolio context. I don't care about what I own individually nearly as much as I do about how my TOTAL PORTFOLIO is doing. To use a sports analogy, I care about the team winning, not who my star players are.

To bring that point home, there's an old story about a player demanding a big raise after his team finished at the bottom of the standings. The team owner's response: "We finished last with you; we can finish last without you."

I realize this is not a very popular approach in this era of "stock picks." And I'll now proceed to call out the best- and worst-looking stocks among the top 20 as I see them currently. But that context should help as you consider if you want to ask me questions about the process and analysis described here.

Charting the "Best" and "Worst" (My Way)

Now, to the charts.

Best-looking Charts Within the 20 Largest S&P 500 Stocks

Apple (AAPL)

As they say, beggars can't be choosy. Most stocks look terrible or undecided. AAPL made the top 3 out of 20, mainly on its "less bad" picture. That PPO momentum indicator at the bottom shows signs of re-accelerating. But that might be good for $10 a share, or just a few percentage points of gain. Beyond that, it might end up joining the rest of them in Mudville, so to speak.

Broadcom (AVGO)

10x forward earnings, and the quarter not being announced for another few weeks. That qualifies as relatively good news, given the messy charts I see elsewhere. So again, "less bad" makes the list here. AVGO is down 17% in 2 months. The way this market trades, that alone can be a rallying cry for the buy-the-dip cohort. The 20-day moving average just went positive for the first time since Thanksgiving time. So that's helpful.

Visa (V)

Rounding out the good list, aka the "less bad" list, here's V. Now, here's the double-edged sword on this one. $320 has been a strong "support" level. Translation: Buyers have repeatedly come in around that price. Earnings are out of the way for this quarter, having been posted in late January. So those 2 items are on the plus side.

But once again, how much upside is here? $360? $370? Not enough to make me a long-term investor in this stock at this point. I'll trade anything, but that's a whole other topic and not the focus of this article.

Worst-looking Charts Within the 20 Largest S&P 500 Stocks

One of my favorite companies (I see them a few times a day at my door, it seems) is not my favorite stock right now. I was seriously looking at it for what is, for me, a rare long-term purchase. But my analysis of the chart held me back. That was in November, around $240 a share.

Now at $204 as of this writing, I think my chances of buying it under $170 are increasing. We'll see. And if that happens, I can see collaring it at first. Then, if all goes well, loosen the hedge. That's how I roll, as they say.

Google (GOOGL)

I guess I won't be writing an article titled "best buys among the Mag-7" any time soon! GOOGL makes the list too. Microsoft (MSFT) gets honorable mention, since it is already down so much.

Here, the signs were there. What do I mean? Strong stocks become weak stocks, first by decelerating in the pace of their advance. Then, often there's a flattish period, as bulls and bears fight it out. At some point, the tug-of-war resolves itself. For GOOGL, the tipoff to me was the 20-day moving average dipping, along with the PPO. Now it's broken the 200-day moving average too. As one Wall Street pundit famously says, "Nothing good happens under the 200-day moving average." There's a lot of work to do here to stop this mini-drop from becoming a much bigger one.

Eli Lilly (LLY)

This one's for NFL fans. The irony I see in LLY making this list is that if it continues to leak (The Colts, an NFL team, play in Lucas Oil Stadium in Indianapolis, where LLY is based), this stock could go the way of the Colts season. They started out as the best team in the league and failed to make the playoffs.

LLY is so news-driven; given demand trends for its GLP-1 drug leadership, I have to listen to what the price chart is telling me. The PPO peaked in early December. And while the price resisted that technical overvaluation (higher than any point in the past 5 years on that indicator), we could look back and see this picture as the point at which the air really started to come out of the bubble. LLY has lost its $1 trillion market cap, and while any stock can go up at any time, the weight of the (technical) evidence points down here.

Final Thoughts for Now

For those following me for a long time (thank you!) or those just recently discovering my work here (thank you too!), I enjoy all constructive and insightful feedback. But I'll go a step further here.

We all know Seeking Alpha has a long, rich history as a go-to platform for crowdsourced fundamental research. I am certainly not the only chartist/technician/price-based analyst here. But this article, as much as any I've written here (more than 700 of them now), is "me" as an investor. I'm not a fundamental type. And the world does not need more good ones; there are many here.

So if you learned some things here about how to use charts...in simple terms, not Greek language stuff...let me know. If I thought this audience would benefit from my doing more like this (take a stock group, analyze the quant factors quickly, then chart the ones that stand out to me), I'd double up the effort to do more of them.

For now, the key thing I learned in reviewing the top 20 stocks, which represent a big piece of the S&P 500, is that there's no clear path to much higher index gains. At least, not yet.

data.title: Ford: Better Value Than You'd Think
data.description:
Ford (F) handily outpaced top-line expectations for its fourth quarter due to strong demand for the automaker’s ICE vehicles. The company missed on earnings, however, as it recorded one-time restructuring charges in its EV business. Following in the footsteps of General Motors (GM), Ford also submitted a strong outlook for adjusted EBIT and free cash flow, as both auto companies expect robust demand for their gas-powered vehicles.

Ford is on track to see a significant rebound in EBIT and cash flow in 2026, driven by Ford Blue, as the company is pivoting away from its loss-making EV business... which contributed to a full-year GAAP loss of $8.2B in 2025. Shares of Ford have upside revaluation potential in 2026 if the U.S. economy remains in good shape and the automaker can sustain its growth momentum in its core business, Ford Blue.

Previous Rating

I rated shares of Ford a strong buy in October — Strong Core Business Performance — because I liked the automaker’s momentum in the gas and hybrid vehicle market. Despite a fire at a supplier's plant affecting production and leading to reduced full-year guidance, Ford is benefiting from growing momentum regarding Ford Blue and Ford Pro... which are two cash cows for the company.

Ford’s Q4 and full-year results were negatively impacted by the firm's pivot to electric vehicles, which resulted in billions in restructuring charges. Investors that can look past the red ink should be able to see a strong value proposition, however: Ford is set for a year of strong profitability, driven mainly by Ford Blue. I like the earnings and free cash flow guidance for 2026 and expect EVs to be less of a profit drag this year than in 2025.

EV Charges, Possibility of Ford Blue Rebound in 2026

Ford beat top-line estimates for the fourth quarter on Tuesday, although the EV business was responsible for the company missing earnings estimates: Ford had non-GAAP earnings of $0.13, which missed the consensus expectation by $0.06 per share. The revenue figure came in at $42.5B and beat the estimate by $670.5M.

High Losses Driven by EV Pivot, Restructuring

Ford benefited from strong demand for its gas-powered vehicles, especially with regard to Bronco and Explorer models in 2025. Demand for non-EV vehicles lifted Ford’s full-year revenues to a fifth consecutive record, and they were published at $187.3B, showing 1% year-over-year growth.

However, the automaker is seeing pressure on its EV sales, which is not new information for investors that have followed Ford's growth in this segment: in the month of January, according to the latest sales update, Ford suffered a 25% drop-off in total EV sales (including hybrids) compared to the year-earlier period. This drop in EV sales, which is in large part related to the expiration of EV tax incentives at the end of Q3'25, is the reason why the company reported billions in impairment-related charges in FY 2025.

As in past quarters, Ford generated strong underlying profitability in Ford Blue and Ford Pro, which are the company's two main earnings drivers. Ford Blue, which consolidates the automaker’s non-EV activities, generated $1.2B in profitability, while Ford Pro, which handles the company’s commercial, government, and fleet customers, generated a segment profit of $1.2B.

The one segment that stood out negatively again was Ford Model e, which includes all of Ford’s EV initiatives: this business reported yet again a loss of $1.2B.

Importantly, because the automaker pivoted away from electric vehicles, Ford incurred $15.5B in non-recurring charges in the fourth quarter, including $10.7B related directly to “Model e Asset Impairment and EV Program Cancellations.” Ford generated a total loss of $8.2B in FY 2025 due to these charges, but the outlook for FY 2026 is strong nonetheless, thanks to a strong U.S. economy and robust demand for ICE vehicles.

Importantly, Ford Blue is in a strong position to deliver EBIT growth this year. The main reason is that a major fire at a supplier impacted this segment last year, which forced the company to lower its guidance. Ford Blue generated a full-year profit of $3.0B compared to $5.3B in FY 2024, so the segment suffered a considerable drop of 43% Y/Y. With Ford's fire-related production issues now out of the way — the issue with Novelis has been fully resolved — I expect a full rebound of Ford Blue's performance in 2026, which I would expect could be a major performance boost for Ford's shares as well.

In terms of guidance, Ford expects to shave off $1.0B in industrial costs in the current fiscal year and targets between $8.0B and $10.0B in adjusted EBIT, up from $6.8B in FY 2025. At the midpoint of guidance, this outlook implies a massive 32% year-over-year growth.

In addition, Ford sees 5.0-6.0B in free cash flow, compared to just $3.5B in FY 2025, implying a material rebound of 57% Y/Y. In other words, the expected absence of restructuring charges in the EV division and strong wholesale/revenue momentum for gas-powered vehicles imply considerable year-over-year growth potential in key metrics that I see as a catalyst for Ford's shares.

Specifically, I see the following catalysts for Ford in 2026:

  • A normalization of Ford Blue's EBIT picture given the absence of new supplier disruptions
  • Continuing strength in ICE wholesale volumes, which led Ford to post record revenue in FY 2025
  • The strategic pivot away from loss-making EV operations is set to boost the firm's overall productivity and earnings
  • New product releases in Ford Blue — new Explorer and Bronco models are set to be released this year — which could further help the rebound in the Ford Blue division that I expect for FY 2026.

Ford’s Valuation

Shares of Ford are currently trading at a price-to-earnings ratio, based off of FY 2027 expected earnings, of 7.5X, which makes them approximately 28% more expensive than those of GM. General Motors is trading at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 5.8X and also submitted a strong earnings scorecard for the fourth quarter, mainly due to the announcement of new stock buybacks: A Strong Buy Despite All-Time Highs. General Motors, like Ford, suffered from restructuring charges in 2025 — in the amount of $7.6B — which was also related to the rightsizing of its EV division.

I believe Ford could trade up to a ~10X P/E ratio, under the assumption that the enterprise maintains its free cash flow guidance, avoids further negative tariff impacts, and EV restructuring charges. Ford also said it will migrate towards a universal EV platform, which in the long term will lower EV production costs. A 10.0X earnings multiplier calculates to a fair value in the region of $18.19, which would give shares of Ford approximately 34% upside revaluation potential.

Risks With Ford

Ford is seeing headwinds in the EV segment, which means the company could see falling EV volumes and incur more restructuring charges and asset impairments in 2026. The main reason for those headwinds relates to the expiration of federal tax incentives on September 30, 2025, after which consumers no longer received the $7,500 tax credit for the purchase of an EV. What I see as the biggest risk is weakening profitability in Ford Blue, which is responsible for driving Ford's earnings growth.

Final Thoughts

Ford submitted a mixed earnings report card for the fourth fiscal quarter on Tuesday: EV charges negatively impacted Ford (and General Motors), which weighed on short-term profitability, but the auto company's revenue performance was great. Ford Blue, despite a drop-off in adjusted EBIT Y/Y, was still widely profitable, and the outlook for this specific segment is favorable, in my opinion. The U.S. economy is doing well right now, while inflation is moderating. This is supportive of credit-financed auto purchases and creates a catalyst for a strong rebound for Ford Blue in the current fiscal year. With Ford Blue's operating conditions set to improve this year and EVs likely going to be less of an earnings drag, I see Ford as a promising rebound investment in 2026.

data.title: AppLovin: This Sale Won't Last
data.description:
Shares of AppLovin Corporation (APP) have gotten absolutely hammered in recent weeks. This high flyer is a name we endorsed as a somewhat speculative play back in April of 2025 at BAD BEAT Investing, where we seek to find mispriced equities set to make a run. After enjoying a great run-up, shares have gone on sale.

This stock and the underlying company's operations checked off many of the boxes we look for. But there is some confusion behind what it does if you go by the name alone. This company is a bit behind the scenes of many different mobile apps and, in particular, mobile games. While they were once heavily focused on creating and publishing their own mobile titles, the company underwent a strategic shift to become more of a pure-play AI software powerhouse. Of course, despite the AI component, as we know, software has absolutely taken it on the chin, causing traders to lose sizable chunks of capital during this route.

But we like AppLovin here, and we view it as cheap, and recently a short-seller even apologized for getting this one wrong. We like what they are doing and think fears are overblown. We are impressed with their core technology, which is led by an engine called AXON. It acts sort of like a rapid matchmaker for the mobile world by connecting app developers with the right target customers to download their apps and further helps those same developers make money by showing the most relevant ads to those users.

So, it’s in a way a data play, as the tech is using advanced data processing to make sure that digital advertising is as efficient as possible. While customers were for a long time mobile gaming studios, ranging from small independent creators to the largest players in the industry, they have successfully branched out to work with customers in ecommerce, fintech, streaming services, and more. AppLovin helps them acquire new users without blowing money trying to acquire people who are not at all interested. It connects the customers to their target markets and does so effectively. It is a high-margin model, as the company earns fees and commissions from advertising spend that flows through their tools. And last we checked, the mobile app space is only set to get larger and larger.

This selloff has knocked the valuation to reasonable levels for the growth at hand. We rate shares a buy once again, especially under $400, and we view the recent Q4 performance as justifying this rating. Specifically, the data in the Q4 financial statement show immense growth. Let us discuss.

We linked to the growth quants above. A and A+ rated all around, while the valuation metrics have also drastically improved given the massive selloff. But given the huge growth, shares are pretty cheap here based on past and future expected growth. Make no mistake, AppLovin had a massive 2025, really highlighting how much their shift toward AI-driven software and connecting app developers and advertisers to the right target customers has paid off for the business. Looking at Q4 alone, revenue jumped to $1.66 billion, which was a huge 66% increase compared to Q4 2024. And for the year as a whole, well, total revenue hit $5.48 billion, which was a 70% growth rate over the $3.22 billion they reported for 2024.

As mentioned above, the AXON technology, which we really like, was a huge driver of these gains. In our opinion, it will continue to drive better results for their advertising partners and will allow the company to successfully expand into new high-growth areas further.

What is perhaps even more impressive is how that revenue growth translated into massive profits through their software-led model. We mentioned that this is a pretty high-margin business. When you look at the $1.66 billion in revenue and find the cost of the revenue line in the report, it was just $168 million. Folks, that is an 89% gross margin. That is stunning. With tech companies like this, we often hone in on the adjusted EBITDA figure, and that reached $1.40 billion, a whopping 82% increase year-over-year. The adjusted EBITDA margin is among the highest in the tech space, with that margin hitting 84 in Q4, a 700 basis point improvement from 77% a year ago. And for all of 2025, the adjusted EBITDA hit $4.51 billion, which was an 87% increase compared to 2024.

When you look at the bottom line earnings, the numbers are just as striking as the revenue growth and EBITDA growth. Net income for Q4 came in at $1.10 billion, which was up 85% compared to the prior year, and the EPS figure hit $3.24 for the quarter. That is strong. And for all of 2025, net income more than doubled, hitting $3.43 billion, while EPS came in at $9.75, a huge jump from the $4.53 the company reported for the previous year.

On the balance sheet side of things, the company is in good shape in our opinion. The company ended the year in an incredibly strong cash position, with $2.49 billion in cash and cash equivalents, a major increase from the $697 million held at the end of 2024. Folks, this is a cash-generating machine now. The company produced $3.95 billion in free cash flow throughout the year. And the company has been buying back shares too, using $2.58 billion to repurchase 6.4 million shares during 2025. We expect more repurchases in the future to continue. There is some debt, but it is very manageable. Long-term debt remained at about $3.51 billion, but given their massive cash flow generation, the company is in a great position. What about as we look ahead?

Looking ahead to the start of 2026, we expect continued strong growth. We view the shares as on sale here and very reasonably valued for the growth. We do not think the massive selloff is warranted here. Rather, we view APP stock as being in a situation where a lower tide sunk all boats, for lack of a better analogy. Software anything got nailed.

But the company is not showing signs of slowing down, even during what is typically a slower seasonal period for the mobile industry here in Q1. For Q1, management sees revenue of $1.745 billion and $1.775 billion, which would represent about 5% to 7% growth sequentially and 19% growth from Q1 of last year. Sure, the wildfire numbers of growth are coming up on tough comps, so it is getting harder to put up the massive percent change beats, but the growth is still wildly strong. The company also expects to maintain their high levels of profitability, forecasting adjusted EBITDA between $1.465 billion and $1.495 billion for the first quarter, which would keep their EBITDA margins steady at approximately 84%.

The current consensus is for $15.59 in EPS here in 2026. That would be 59.9% growth from the 2025 EPS. At 29 times FWD earnings per share with the strong margins in place and massive free cash flow, APP shares are a bargain under $400.

data.title: NexGen Energy Is Accelerating Its Entry Into Uranium Mining In Saskatchewan
data.description:
NexGen Energy's Bullish Ascent: Nuclear Revolution Looms

We are updating our analysis of NexGen Energy (NXE), a uranium stock traded on the NYSE, and since our previous coverage, there has been progress in the company’s entry into uranium mining, marking a significant step forward in the company’s mining plans.

The momentum has continued to build from there. The market is rightly excited about this nuclear project, and now our readers must stay ahead of the crowd and not be conveyed to the tail end of the growth wave. Many may still be acclimating to what is taking shape on the horizon. At this crucial juncture, we believe that participants need an update on their journey.

Given the favorable market and uranium industry conditions and the signs of openness and cooperation in the Canadian region’s communities that they have shown or prompted optimistic assumptions, we will focus in this analysis on the reinforcement of these that has occurred in the interim. These strengthen the arguments for our confirmation of a BUY rating and signal a stock with the potential to move forward in the much-desired direction.

NXE Is Gradually Coming Into Focus for Bullish Analysts

NexGen Energy and Its Flagship Asset, Rook 1 in Saskatchewan

We will not repeat the locational, mineralogical, and economic characteristics of NexGen Energy’s flagship asset, Rook 1, as this, along with other data relevant to its optimal development into the world’s largest low-cost uranium mine—intended to provide clean energy for the future—has already been discussed in our previous article. In that analysis, we also highlighted the expertise of the NexGen Energy team and referenced the feasibility study conducted under N.I. 43-101. Readers who wish to learn more or refresh their knowledge of the aforementioned characteristics of NexGen Energy’s Rook 1 mine are encouraged to read our previous article.

In this analysis, we will pick up from where we left off on November 11, 2025. Since then, significant progress has been made on Rook 1, advancing on various fronts and bringing it closer to the start of construction. This underground uranium mine, located 131 km north of La Loche, Saskatchewan, is on its way to becoming a full-scale production facility. Meanwhile, the market’s focus on uranium as a clean energy source is considerable right now.

As covered in our earlier analysis, we also highlighted the long-term benefits of Rook 1, particularly for the economy and well-being of communities in Saskatchewan. NexGen Energy’s Rook-I proposal is aligned with global market goals of transitioning to a carbon-neutral economy, based on secure energy sources for prosperity and evolution, while respecting the environment and the dignity of the communities where the natural resources are located.

Our Previous Coverage: A Practical Summary For Our Readers

From our previous article: the 35,065-hectare uranium mine, wholly owned by NexGen, is located in the Athabasca Basin, a renowned mining district, which contains high-grade uranium deposits. It lies in northern Saskatchewan, a province that supplies approximately 20% of the world's uranium. Demand for uranium is expected to continue rising due to the global trend toward expanding nuclear power plants.

NexGen Energy's Rook 1 uranium project could generate substantial cash flow thanks to long-term upward pressure on uranium prices. A global capital increase of C$950 million appeared to mitigate some risks but did not eliminate existing uncertainties in the event of delays. Rook 1's attractive net present value (NPV) and other key financial metrics make NXE stock a promising investment.

Globally Coveted Uranium From Nuclear Power and AI Boom

The company’s February 2026 presentation statistics from the WNA World Nuclear Fuel Report 2025 – September 5, 2025 – and McKinsey & Co., August 2025 – “Scaling bigger, faster, cheaper data centers with smarter designs” confirm the ascent of uranium as a feedstock for global energy production from nuclear power plants: Global capacity is projected to increase from 380 GW to 1,200 GW by 2050. The main drivers will be: AI, electrification, and electric vehicles. These power plants will require uranium – and in even greater quantities.

The Structural Deficit in the Uranium Market

Uranium prices will remain high in the long term, as analysts believe that a supply of just over 200 million pounds will not fill the gap in the rapidly growing hunt for uranium, exceeding 500 million pounds by 2040.

Rook 1 from NexGen Energy: The Marketable

A volume of 239.6 million pounds of U₃O₈ is contained in 4.575 million tonnes of ore, with an average U₃O₈ grade of 2.37%, with a trend towards higher concentrations in high concentration zones, as highlighted on March 5, 2021, by Mining Technology. Rook 1 will thus produce an average of 21.7 million pounds of U₃O₈ per year.

The entire uranium complex centered around the extraction and processing of uranium ore from the Arrow deposit. There, mineralization begins at a depth of about 100 meters, originates in a crystalline basement system, and extends to a depth of 950 meters.

Regulatory Approval Process for the Rook 1: The Current Status

NexGen Energy is still awaiting approval from the Canadian Nuclear Safety Commission ("CNSC") before construction can begin on the Rook 1 underground uranium production property. Since our initial article on NexGen Energy stock, the CNSC has been conducting hearings to assess the proposal and its suitability for the public and environmental interests, particularly the economic and social well-being of local communities and groups, as well as the wider Canadian region. The second phase of the hearings is currently underway, following the successful completion of the first phase on November 19, 2025. This provides stakeholders and the public with an opportunity to share their views on Rook 1’s headway with the commission.

On Monday, February 9, 2026, during Part 2 of the CNSC hearing for the Rook 1 scheme approval, NexGen Energy stated (via Newsfile Corp.):

This exciting milestone represents the final step in the Federal regulatory approval process for the Rook 1 Project. It concludes more than 12 years of disciplined, comprehensive environmental and social studies, and highlights NexGen's transparent and genuine engagement approach with local and Indigenous communities that has delivered measurable, positive and generational benefits since 2013.

Sentiment Lifts on Exploration and Uranium Resource Progression

The uranium deposit in the Patterson Corridor East (“PCE”) exhibits high uranium prospects in terms of continuity and concentration, living up to the world-class reputation of the Arrow deposit (whose production capacity and recoverable resources we have already highlighted).

The continued enormous possibility for upturn is coming out as PCE's drilling results are evaluated. As the data confirms this, as well as the pending approval of the Arrow deposit by the Canadian federal authorities, these two should have a very positive impact on the share price. In a comment on NexGen's announcement of eye-catching high-grade assay results at the Patterson Corridor East on November 12, 2025, at 6:30 a.m. ET, Jason Craven, Vice President of Exploration, stated (Newsfile Corp.):

[...] Importantly, [...]. The repeatability of consistent high-grade results at PCE, with so much untested area remaining highlights the significant potential for continued expansion.

After more than 20 years exploring for uranium in the Basin, I never imagined I'd get the chance to work on a project like Arrow - something that was thought to be impossible prior to NexGen. To now find ourselves at the early stages of another discovery with similar characteristics and growing potential is both remarkable and deeply humbling.

Mineralization Can Still Be Unlocked in 2026

Last month, NexGen Energy announced the start of its 2026 exploration program, and with over 45,000 meters of planned drilling, there is more than enough material to bolster market confidence in NXE's stock and offer the chance as well to mitigate, though not entirely eliminate, the risk of delays in obtaining final approval and authorization from the federal government for Rook 1. In a comment on NexGen's announcement of […] Commencement of 2026 Exploration Program on Jan. 15, 2026, at 6:30 AM ET, Jason Craven, Vice President of Exploration, stated (Newsfile Corp.):

Last year's highly transitional and successful program positions NexGen to enter 2026 with an even deeper understanding of this broad and growing high-grade system. A multitude of targets remain to be tested as we continue to systematically advance PCE with a dual priority track of expanding both the high-grade subdomains as well as the overall mineralized system. [...] with uranium in the face of mounting and persistent deficits.

Further exploration initiatives are also providing pleasant indications of new, uranium-rich deposits, for example, at Todd Lake and Grey Island.

As these exploration results underscore the on-the-make that may lie hidden within the company's mineral portfolio – whether through possible broadening of the Patterson Lake Corridors’ prospects or, as in the case of Grey Island, through the largely unexplored land areas in the southern Athabasca Basin – they will be important drivers of the share price in a more and more uranium-focused market.

Powering the Future: Domestic Uranium and AI-Driven Nuclear Plants

NexGen's moving forward with permitting, exploration, and resource expansion positions the company as a key player in the uranium sector. As one of the few providers attempting to close the dramatic supply deficit created by geopolitical factors, a nuclear resurgence, the crescive attention to national uranium security, and modernizations such as AI-empowered data centers—which are accelerating interest in uranium exploitation—NexGen Energy just needs to capitalize on this wind.

NexGen Energy Ltd. on the NYSE

NexGen Energy Ltd.’s NYSE-listed shares under the symbol “NXE” were trading at $11.72/share, giving it a market capitalization of $7.72 billion, as of the writing of the article.

Technical Analysis

The current share price is 31.2% above the 52-week middle point of $8.935 per share, and the 52-week range is $3.91 to $13.96 per share. The 20-day moving average is above the 50-day moving average, which in turn is above the 100-day moving average, which is above the 200-day moving average. This indicates that market sentiment perfectly aligns with analyst opinions on NexGen Energy. In the last three months, 15 out of 17 analyst recommendations have been buy recommendations. Undeniably this momentum is confirmed by short-, medium-, and long-term signals.

In our opinion, these drifts are entirely justified. Anyone who disagrees is, in our view, acting irrationally. They have limited understanding of how this energy sector is evolving, with augmenting sympathy for nuclear power for national sovereignty to meet the rising hunger for energy from cutting-edge technologies like AI, and how quickly NexGen Energy is preparing to become a strategic uranium supplier for North American nuclear power plants.

Valuation

At the time of this writing, uranium futures in the US were trading at US$86.25 per pound, according to Trading Economics.

This translates to the following key figures for the Rook 1 scheme:

  • a net present value (NPV) of C$5.365 billion (US$3.941 billion).
  • an average annual cash flow of C$1.72 billion (US$1.263 billion) (over the first five years).
  • an internal rate of return (IRR) of 41.5% and a payback period of 1.15 years.

The figures on the right side of the table, titled “Interim Trend Update (Q4-2023 Dollars),” incorporate updated cost estimates, inflation, and other economic factors (as of Q4 2023), thus providing a more current and realistic assessment of the NPV, as well as other key performance indicators.

This leads to two key metrics:

  • The market capitalization-to-net present value (NPV) ratio is 2.

And,

  • The market capitalization-to-average annual cash flow ratio of 6.11x. This already makes the valuation of NexGen Energy shares appear more enticing than the sector median’s Price/Cash Flow (TTM) of 6.73.

However,

NexGen Energy's stock valuation appears even more promising to us, as the positive development of the uranium market and its strong fundamentals are likely to drive uranium prices significantly higher than current forecasts. This will further improve the situation considerably.

Analysts even predict a rise in the uranium price to US$90–US$100 per pound in 2026.

  • The Net Present Value (NPV) of Rook 1 is expected to increase to C$6.79 billion (US$4.987 billion), a 27% increase over the current uranium price scenario.
  • The average annual cash flow is expected to increase to C$2.04 billion (or US$1.5 billion) (over the first five years), a 19% increase.
  • The internal rate of return (IRR) is expected to rise to 46.9%, an increase of 540 basis points.
  • The amortization period is expected to shorten, meaning an earlier amortization of the initial capital investment, to 1 year instead of 1 year and 2 months.

Possible Scenario:

  • The market capitalization-to-net present value (NPV) ratio is 1.5, down 25% if uranium prices rise by 16%, preparing for favorable repercussions for the market sentiment. Therefore, NXE shares on the NYSE look more attractively valued for positioning oneself within the context of the Rook 1 development.
  • The market capitalization-to-average annual cash flow ratio of 5.15x, which makes the valuation of NexGen Energy shares appear even more enticing than the sector median’s Price/Cash Flow (FWD) of 6.77.

The strategic shift towards a stable domestic uranium supply and the impending start of an era of massive energy consumption, driven by artificial intelligence, appear to place no limits on the uranium price. This is leading to increasingly positive developments in the market capitalization-to-NPV ratio on the NYSE and the market capitalization-to-average annual cash flow ratio – both important valuation metrics. This is further supported, among other things, by the current weakness of the US dollar against the Canadian dollar at the moment, under the administration of US President Donald Trump.

In light of the above, NXE’s stock seems well-positioned for growth, with the potential to outperform in the coming period, particularly if uranium prices continue to rise and economic factors remain favorable, including a weaker US dollar. When we last analyzed NXE (NexGen Energy), the price of uranium was US$78.136 per pound. Since then, it has risen by 10.4% to its current price of US$86.25, resulting in an approximately 11% increase in net present value (NPV) and approximately 5.7% in cash flow. NXE's market capitalization has increased by approximately 35.5% to $11.72 per share.

Based on the aforementioned metrics and historical market performance, a rise in the uranium price to $100 (analyst forecast for 2026) – which would lead to a 16.3% increase in net present value (NPV) – could boost the share price by 40% to 55%, assuming continued positive market sentiment. This would place the share price in a range of $16.40 to $18.15, consistent with Wall Street's upper price target of $17.68 per share as of this writing (and consistent with recommendations of 15 Buys).

The Risks

According to the Mining Technology website (see above for the link), Rook-1 requires an upfront disbursement of approximately C$1.3 billion (US$954.84 million, as of this writing) with a planned mine life of 10.7 years. The company presentation from February 2026 indicates total liquidity for NexGen Energy of C$1.2 billion. Additional funds may come from project financing agreements, and securing these should not prove challenging given the growing market enthusiasm in NexGen’s Rook 1. Liquidity will decrease, as approximately C$200 million is spent annually on operations and investments, including resource utilization and capitalization of pre-construction and exploration activities (see operating cash flow plus cash flow from investing activities for the LTM September 2025).

We expect that the final permits to start construction of the Rook-1 mine will not be long in coming and that financial resources cannot dry up dramatically in the meantime. This can at least be inferred from the optimism radiated by the press releases about the strides in federal permitting and the prevailing view that the establishment of domestic nuclear power generation in North America is of great, if not crucial, importance. Then we must also take into account the 2.7 million pounds of uranium stored in the Strategic Uranium Reserve inventory of NXE, which, valued at current prices, corresponds to gross proceeds of US$232.875 million or C$317.008 million. There is little chance that NexGen Energy will run out of money during the journey, including the construction of the uranium mine.

Another risk is the volatility of commodity prices. Although researchers consider this fear unfounded, the image of a nuclear power plant—especially given the war in Ukraine, where the Zaporizhzhia nuclear power plant is surrounded by unstable conditions—evokes strong anxieties: fear that if disturbances occur, as we can never rule out anything, these could raise alarms about danger to the local population and the environment.

This may seem negligible given the technology currently in use, but the market can take advantage of this if it wishes; for example, if at a certain point it sees greater opportunities in renewable energy as a more lucrative alternative to uranium-fueled nuclear power. In such a case, uranium might no longer be sponsored as critically as it is now, and the price could fall accordingly. A drastic drop in uranium prices could shock the economic viability and profitability chances of Rook 1, as well as market mood surrounding NexGen Energy's NYSE shares. And of course, delays in obtaining the permits required to start construction of the mine could negatively affect market sentiment as well.

Conclusion

NexGen Energy Ltd. is making good progress in discovering the uranium deposits at its Rook 1 project in southwestern Saskatchewan's Athabasca Basin. The strategic shift towards national uranium security and breakthrough technologies like artificial intelligence contribute greatly to the idea that we need to massively lift electricity generation, and to do this we cannot help but rely more aggressively on the construction and operation of nuclear power plants. This stock deserves a bullish rating, which is fully justified.

The company has the financial resources for Rook 1 and can easily acquire additional assets.

data.title: Grab: The Best Super App In Asia
data.description:
Introduction

Grab Holdings Limited (GRAB) has been a recent addition to my portfolio, and I want to take the chance of the company’s recent earnings to start a coverage. Fiscal 2025 has been marked as the structural transition from a venture-incentivized growth engine to a self-sustaining, profitable regional hegemon. The firm has delivered its first full year of net profit, and I believe that Grab could be one of the best opportunities in 2026 as it confirms the scalability of the superapp model in emerging markets.

Current Dynamics

Grab posted a mixed set of earnings based solely on the big numbers, as Q4 EPS Non-GAAP came in at $0.04, a beat of 3 cents and the highest level seen. While revenue came in at $906MM, a slight miss of $34.6MM but again, a quarterly record for the company. The company also crossed the 50MM monthly transacting user (MTU) threshold, which means that the platform has achieved a level of ecosystem density that now enables it to extract high-margin revenue through financial services and advertising, while maintaining a dominant share in the mass-market daily essentials category.

The results essentially demonstrate a robust reacceleration of growth paired with disciplined margin expansion. Full year revenue reached a record $3.37B, a 20% increase Y/Y and this was underpinned by a 21% surge in On-Demand Gross Merchandise Value (GMV), which hit $22.1B, suggesting that the platform is not only capturing more users but is also becoming a more integral part of those users’ daily spending habits.

To be fair, the most striking aspect of the 2025 performance was the dramatic improvement of the bottom line for Grab. The company swung from a net loss of $105MM in 2024 to a net profit of $200MM in 2025, and represents a $358MM Y/Y improvement in the bottom line. The company also drastically expanded Adj. EBITDA, which grew by 60% Y/Y to reach $500MM for the full year. The fourth quarter alone generated $148MM in EBITDA, marking the 16 th consecutive quarter of expansion in this measure.

On the topic of profitability, the company was also able to generate $290MM FCF for the full year, up drastically from 2024, providing a significant buffer for strategic reinvestment and shareholder returns. The management’s decision to authorize a new $500MM serves as a strong signal that the era of aggressive cash burn has been replaced by a focus on high-quality, cash-generative growth.

I'll dive deeper into the company’s segments with first mobility that is the high-margin engine of regional urbanization. It has high barriers to entry and a sophisticated supply-side network. The revenue for the segment reached $1.22B, a 16% Y/Y increase while the EBITDA margin expanded to 8.7%, illustrating the effectiveness of the platform’s efforts to drive operational efficiency in a high-inflation environment.

A strong tailwind for the mobility side has been the sustained recovery and growth of regional travel, plus it now has great partnerships with global platforms like AliPay (BABA) and Trip.com which have been instrumental in enhancing brand visibility for international travelers even before they land in Southeast Asia. Thus, traveler MTUs have grown more than 20 times over the last three years, plus airport-related rides account for more than 10% of total mobility GMV, representing a high-margin segment.

The management of driver supply has been a critical factor in maintaining service levels during the surge in volume. The number of active drivers reached new all-time highs in 2025, while driver retention rates remained stable at approx. 90%. By optimizing driver supply and utilizing AI for more efficient matching and dispatching, with over 90% of rides now dispatched through AI, improving reliability for users without negatively impacting the earnings of driver-partners.

On the deliveries segment, it has successfully transitioned from a food-centric service to a comprehensive on-demand logistics platform. In 2025, delivery revenue grew by 21% Y/Y to $1.8B, while GMV reached $14.2B. The segment has demonstrated significant margin resilience, with EBITDA as a percentage of GMV reaching 2.1% in certain quarters. This was in part driven by ad revenue and improvements in logistics efficiency. Furthermore, one of the most promising growth avenues within the deliveries segment is GrabMart, which is currently growing nearly 2 times faster than the core of Grab's food delivery operation. GrabMart users increased by 30% Y/Y during the year, as the platform deepened its integration with major supermarket companies.

Plus, GrabAds has emerged as a high-margin revenue stream that significantly bolsters the deliveries segment’s profitability. Again, strong growth was seen with the segment reported surge of 15% Y/Y, while the average spend per advertiser surged by 41%. The company essentially provided merchants with enterprise-level digital tools to maximize their return on advertising spend, thus deepening merchant loyalty right now.

And finally, on the financial side, it has become the fastest-growing vertical, akin to MeradoLibre, which saw revenue increase by 375 Y/Y to $347MM in 2025. Though EBITDA remains negative, as it continues to scale, management has guided for an EBITDA breakeven target by the second half of 2026. The strategy for this segment is centered on using the platform’s vast transactional data to provide financial products that are both low-cost to acquire and highly personalized to the user. Plus, the total loan portfolio grew by 120% Y/Y, ending at $1.18B, thanks to the adoption among the platform’s drivers, merchants and consumers for whom Grab is often their primary financial interface.

Valuation

Grab is certainly not a bargain example as its P/E Non-GAAP is currently standing at 65.24 and its EV/EBIDA is at 52 right now. Though diving deeper than just metrics, I want to show you how dominant the company is in Southeast Asia, as Grab extended its regional market share to 55%, and that dominance is particularly pronounced in countries such as Singapore, Malaysia, and the Philippines, where it commands around two-thirds of the market. Though in Indonesia, Grab remains in a fierce battle with Gojek (GTOFF), even if a potential merger between the two continues to be a topic of speculation among U.S. investors. A potential merger would create an entity estimated to have 99% market share in the country.

I believe that the best growth prospects for the company are centered on a pivot from growth at all costs to a high-margin, ecosystem-led monetization model. Management has outlined a clear trajectory to triple EBITDA to $1.5B by 2028. More so, Grab is banking on scaling the financial services as well as wealth management, with several critical milestones on the immediate horizon. Grab will aim to exceed $2B in the gross loan book by the end of 2026, which is, in my opinion, highly achievable. Plus, the company just announced the $425MM acquisition of Stash Financial, a move designed to accelerate Grab’s wealth management roadmap, offering millions of users in the region access to equities and managed portfolios.

I also believe that GrabAds is a high-margin hidden lever, which is similar to Amazon's (AMZN) early ad business. Currently, it has only about 3% penetration, leaving ample room for improvement while it increases the margin structure without increasing fees for users or drivers.

Risks

There are some regulatory headwinds right now at Grab, with Indonesian authorities considering a new regulation that would halve the ceiling on ride-hailing commissions from 20% to 10%, which could result in a downside to Grab’s EBITDA in 2026. Plus, new proposals may require platforms to fund full accident and death insurance for drivers and provide additional social protections, further eroding margins.

The company is also facing intense regional competition from the likes of Sea Limited (SE) and Gojek that are spending aggressively on incentives to capture market share. For example, Sea’s ShopeeFood has climbed to become the region’s second-largest player, surpassing Foodpanda in some markets.

Conclusion

Grab is simply a formidable network in the region, and I can vouch for it as I’ve used it firsthand on trips in Southeast Asia. It is so convenient and akin to Uber (UBER) and how it became the go-to app for all delivery and transportation modes. I believe in the future of Grab, and I award it a Strong Buy based on the extremely good quarter.
